text: Resection of allele carcinoid in an obese patient. The sixty-five-year-old BMI-fifty woman with previous hysterectomy was diagnosed with allele carcinoid on imaging and blood tests following investigation of abdominal pain. This coronal CT shows some of the vasculature and the mass. She had very central obesity. Concerns included risks of mesenteric vascular injury and increased risk of incisional hernia.

The surgeon uses a laparoscope with a gel point port and a mini fan and steel incision, along with three 5mm ports. Adhesions from a previous hysterectomy are dissected using a Ligasure device. The large carcinoid mass is visible, causing cicatrization of the mesentery and bowel loops, with adhesions present between the sigmoid colon and pelvis.

The surgeon visualizes a mesenteric nodal mass at the base of the knot, located at the root of the transverse mesocolon. A marker tape is placed at five centimeters to measure the bowel distal and proximal to the mass, revealing over a meter of ileum downstream and a significant length of ileum involved in the mass.

The surgeon measures approximately four meters of bowel upstream to the duodenal jejunal flexure. Livac slings, elasticized bands with hooks, are inserted to help lay out the bowel around the mass. The first generation of the product is shown in use.

The surgeon catches the peritoneum and the peritoneum of the mesentery, applying a second Livac sling with adjustable hooks to improve mesentery exposure. A third Livac sling is applied, enhancing the layout of the mesentery. The transverse mesocolon is defined to locate the proximal superior mesenteric artery (SMA).

The surgeon scores the peritoneum over the small bowel mesentery on the patient's left side of the mass, carefully working to identify the vessels supplying the involved segment of ileum. The fatty peritoneum presents a challenge in visualizing the vessels, but tension on the mesentery aids in the process. The surgeon adjusts the hooks on the Livac slings to maintain optimal tension.

The surgeon navigates through the fatty peritoneal plane of the mesentery, identifying the origin of the right colic vessels and entering the retroperitoneal plane. Dissection progresses towards the intended line of transection on the left side of the tumor mass, revealing vascular anatomy amidst the fatty tissue.

The surgeon checks the length of the small bowel upstream of the mass, using Livac slings to stabilize the mesentery. A vein becomes visible, indicating entry into the correct dissection plane. The surgeon adjusts the hook and begins dissecting towards the posterior leaf of the mesentery, creating an opening in the tissue.

The surgeon identifies the main branch of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) supplying the tumor mass, with the SMA continuing to supply the bowel proximal to the mass. The sizable vessel is visible, indicating the limit of dissection to avoid devascularizing the bowel.

The surgeon uses a Covidien tan cartridge tri-stapler, 30 millimeters, to transect a large vessel associated with the SMA. The vessel is visible extending from the root of the transverse mesocolon towards the bottom of the screen, highlighting the critical need to avoid devascularizing the proximal small bowel.

The surgeon confirms that the section of bowel is not supplied by the ileocolic vessels and proceeds with mesenteric dissection towards the planned proximal line of transection. The hooks and slings used for stabilization are set aside as the layout of the bowels is checked.

The surgeon plans the distal line of transection, with the right colic and ileocolic vessels visible. A combination of clips and ligature is used to dissect the sizable vascular arcades, maintaining a distance from the tumor mass.

The surgeon divides the proximal end of the planned transection using a tan cartridge Covidian tristapler, followed by the distal transection. The specimen, a large section of bowel, is placed into a Covidian fifteen millimeter ender catch bag. The twelve millimeter port is removed from the gel point gel, allowing the catch bag to pass through smoothly. The specimen slides out against the Alexis, and the surgeon re-examines the distal margin for potential additional resection.

The surgeon observes a reasonable length of terminal ileum leading to the ileocecal valve and decides to close the large mesenteric window using a V-lock stitch. The thick and fatty peritoneum of the mesentery is visible, reducing the risk of needle penetration through significant vessels.
The surgeon completes the mesenteric closure using a two-row proline suture to align the bowel. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed in the Barcelona style, following a fan and steel incision.

The surgeon uses a purple, 60mm tri-stapler cartridge to create two enterotomies, aligning the anti-mesenteric borders by pulling on a proline stitch. A transverse firing is completed with a tan cartridge, addressing the vascular supply in the mesentery. At the crutch, a two ovicral crutch stitch is applied to reinforce the area, ensuring it is snug without cutting through the tissue, to relieve tension on the staple line.
The surgeon completes the laparoscopic component of the procedure, followed by closing the fan and steel incision and the five millimeter abdominal ports.
